title: The Organism Seen From the Whole — Steiner Reading Goethe's Morphology
author: Rudolf Steiner / Goethe

body.text: Steiner'sreading of Goethe begins with adistinction. Inorganic phenomena can be understood by external relation — one body strikes another, one condition produces another. The organism requires adifferent eye. Aliving thing is not assembled from parts. Its parts are expressions of awhole.
The great significance of Goethe'smorphological works rests on the fact that in them he was the first to extend the idea of the whole to the study of living organisms.

Steiner calls this ascientific deed because it gives method, result. Goethe found away to study life without reducing it to mechanism. The living being is grasped by seeing how the particular part proceeds from the whole. The imagination does not invent ahidden whole. It learns to perceive the whole through the transformations of the parts.

body.caption: The whole expressing itself through organs — metamorphosis as visible continuity.

body.text: Again the matter is otherwise for Goethe. They complement one another. Goethe'sideas provide the foundation for the other view.

The key phrase is 'inner, ideal relatedness.' Goethe deepens outer similarity into inward kinship. Organs resemble each other because they are inwardly related. The visible rests on an invisible law. The first rendering shows separate plant-parts. The Goethean rendering shows one formative idea moving through them.
Goethe is the Copernicus and Kepler of the organic world.

This is Steiner'sstrongest image. Before Kepler, people saw the heavens. After Kepler, they saw lawful motion. Before Goethe, people saw plants and animals. After Goethe, they could begin to see metamorphosis. The comparison frames imagination as scientific — adiscovery of law where mechanical thinking cannot reach.

body.caption: Kepler of the organic world — lawful motion seen in living form.

body.text: The essential being of the particular itself presses toward the general — the typus.

Every particular being asks to be understood through more than its particularity. Aleaf is this leaf with this edge and colour. But it presses toward the typus — the ideal-general that makes it aleaf within aliving sequence. The Goethean eye holds both: the particular as local appearance of ageneral formative law.

This is the eye that sees livingly. Do not dissolve the particular into abstraction or trap it as isolated fact. Hold both together. The organism is only known when both its here-ness and its lawfulness are perceived in asingle act.
